papers.title: 591 high velocity stars in the Galactic halo selected from LAMOST DR7 and Gaia DR2
papers.author_names: Yin-Bi Li A-Li Luo You-Jun Lu Xue-Sen Zhang Jiao Li Rui Wang Fang Zuo Maosheng Xiang Yuan-Sen Ting T. Marchetti Shuo Li You-Fen Wang Shuo Zhang Kohei Hattori Yong-Heng Zhao Hua-Wei Zhang Gang Zhao

abstract.text: In this paper, we report 591 high velocity star candidates (HiVelSCs) selected from over 10 million spectra of the data release seven (DR7) of the Large Sky Area Multi-object Fiber Spectroscopic Telescope and the second Gaia data release, with three-dimensional velocities in the Galactic rest-frame larger than 445 km/s. We show that at least 43 HiVelSCs are unbound to the Galaxy with escape probabilities larger than 50%, and this number decreases to eight if the possible parallax zero-point error is corrected. Most of these HiVelSCs are metal-poor and slightly alpha-enhanced inner halo stars. Only 14% of them have [Fe/H] > -1, which may be the metal-rich "in situ" stars in halo formed in the initial collapse of the Milky Way or metal-rich stars formed in the disk or bulge but kinematically heated. The low ratio of 14% implies that the bulk of stellar halo was formed from the accretion and tidal disruption of satellite galaxies. In addition, HiVelSCs on the retrograde orbits have a slightly lower metallicities on average compared with these on the prograde orbits, meanwhile metal-poor HiVelSCs with [Fe/H] < -1 have an even faster mean retrograde velocity compared with metal-rich HiVelSCs. To investigate the origins of HiVelSCs, we perform orbit integrations and divide them into four types, i.e., hypervelocity stars, hyper-runaway stars, runaway stars and fast halo stars. A catalog for these 591 HiVelSCs, including radial velocities, atmospheric parameters, Gaia astrometric parameters, spatial positions, and velocities, etc., is available at http://paperdata.chinavo.org/LYB/lamostdr7_gaiadr2_hvs_591.csv.

triage.label: rule-direct
triage.reason: Direct title match: high-velocity or extreme-velocity stars

brief.tldr: [research paper] A large sample of 591 high-velocity star candidates (HiVelSCs) is identified from LAMOST DR7 and Gaia DR2 data, with 3D velocities exceeding 445 km/s in the Galactic rest frame. At least 43 are unbound to the Galaxy (escape probability >50%), dropping to eight after correcting for parallax errors; the population is predominantly metal-poor and α-enhanced, with only 14% having [Fe/H] > −1, supporting a major accretion-driven origin of the stellar halo. Retrograde orbit stars are slightly less metal-rich and exhibit faster mean velocities than prograde ones, and HiVelSCs are classified into four dynamical types—hypervelocity, hyper-runaway, runaway, and fast halo—via orbit integrations, providing a detailed kinematic and dynamical catalog for Galactic halo evolution studies.

source_fields.title: 591 high velocity stars in the Galactic halo selected from LAMOST DR7 and Gaia DR2

source_fields.sections_text: Data: This section describes the complementary use of LAMOST spectroscopic data for stellar parameters and radial velocities, and Gaia astrometric data for precise distances and motions, to jointly determine galactocentric distance and total velocity of stars. Conclusion: This study identifies 591 high-velocity star candidates from the LAMOST-Gaia catalog, confirming their kinematic association with the Galactic halo and showing that most are unbound in various Galactic potential models, with a subset meeting stricter criteria and exhibiting halo-like kinematics. Discussion: Applying a negative parallax zero-point correction of -0.029 mas reduces Galactocentric distances by an average of 0.4 kpc and total velocities by 66 km s⁻¹, decreasing unbound HiVelSCs in the Watkins+2019 model from 43 to 8 and increasing bound stars in the Xue+2008 model from 284 to 492, while stricter astrometric and velocity criteria further reduce the number of unbound stars to just two with precise parallaxes. Introduction: This paper introduces a novel classification and dynamical framework for high velocity stars that precisely distinguishes their origins—such as tidal ejection, binary MBH interactions, and runaway mechanisms—by combining kinematic data with galactic potential models, thereby solving the long-standing problem of unambiguously identifying and attributing the formation mechanisms of these extreme stellar populations. Appendix A Tables: This appendix provides detailed tables listing escape velocities, unbound probabilities, and stellar parameters for 20 of the fastest high-velocity stars in the Galactic halo across seven potential models, along with a comprehensive catalog of 591 such stars. Orbital Integration and Origin: This section uses numerical orbit integrations to show that 246 bound high-velocity stars in the Galactic halo follow highly eccentric orbits with median eccentricity of 0.978 and maximum vertical amplitude of 105 kpc, consistent with their kinematic properties as halo population members. Properties of High Velocity Star Candidates: The 591 high-velocity star candidates are predominantly located beyond the Galactic disk with negative $X$ and $Y$ velocities, showing a significant retrograde motion in $V_y$ that suggests a possible extragalactic origin.

To investigate the origins of HiVelSCs, we perform orbit integrations and divide them into four types, i.e., hypervelocity stars, hyper-runaway stars, runaway stars and fast halo stars.
title: 591 high velocity stars in the Galactic halo selected from LAMOST DR7 and Gaia DR2

catalog_assessment.evidence: Title explicitly states '591 high velocity stars...selected from LAMOST DR7 and Gaia DR2'. Abstract describes selection from over 10 million spectra, provides detailed object-level data (velocities, metallicities, orbital classifications), and includes a direct link to a downloadable catalog CSV file with radial velocities, atmospheric parameters, Gaia astrometry, positions, and velocities.

papers.title: Runaway blue main-sequence stars at high Galactic latitudes. Target selection with Gaia and spectroscopic identification
papers.author_names: Roberto Raddi Andreas Irrgang Ulrich Heber David Schneider Simon Kreuzer

abstract.text: Motivated by the historical identification of runaway main-sequence (MS) stars of early spectral type at high Galactic latitudes, we test the capability of Gaia at identifying new such stars. We have selected ~2300 sources with Gaia magnitudes of GBP - GRP < 0.05, compatible with the colors of low-extinction MS stars earlier than mid-A spectral type, and obtained low-resolution optical spectroscopy for 48 such stars. By performing detailed photometric and spectroscopic analyses, we derive their atmospheric and physical parameters (effective temperature, surface gravity, radial velocity, interstellar reddening, spectrophotometric distance, mass, radius, luminosity, and age). The comparison between spectrophotometric and parallax-based distances enables us to disentangle the MS candidates from older blue horizontal branch (BHB) candidates. We identify 12 runaway MS candidates, with masses between 2 and 6 Msun. Their trajectories are traced back to the Galactic disc to identify their most recent Galactic plane crossings and the corresponding flight times. All 12 candidates are ejected from the Galactic disc within 2 to 16.5 kpc from the Galactic center and possess flight times that are shorter than their evolutionary ages, compatible with a runaway hypothesis. Three MS candidates have ejection velocities exceeding 450 km/s, thus, appear to challenge the canonical ejection scenarios for late B-type stars. The fastest star of our sample also has a non-negligible Galactic escape probability if its MS nature can be confirmed. We identify 27 BHB candidates, and the two hottest stars in our sample are rare late O and early B type stars of low mass evolving towards the white dwarf cooling sequence.

brief.skipped_reason: weak rule match; retained with search metadata only

source_fields.sections_text: Footnotes: The section lists citations and references for various astronomical surveys and databases used in the study. Conclusions: This study identifies and analyzes early-type main sequence runaway star candidates using Gaia data, finds three stars with ejection velocities exceeding classical models' limits, and highlights the need for high-resolution spectroscopy and future Gaia data to better understand their origins and ejection mechanisms. Introduction: This paper presents a novel identification and characterization of blue main sequence runaway stars at high Galactic latitudes using Gaia data, addressing the problem of understanding the origin and ejection mechanisms of these stars beyond the classical binary supernova and dynamical ejection scenarios. Observations: This section describes the observational strategy, setup, and data reduction methods used to obtain high-quality spectroscopic data for a sample of early-type stars, with careful attention to target selection, instrumental performance, and radial velocity accuracy. Data analysis: The analysis employs a three-step approach using spectral energy distributions and synthetic model grids to derive atmospheric parameters, with separate models for main-sequence and horizontal-branch stars to account for differences in chemical composition and stellar nature. Target selection: This section describes a targeted selection of massive, blue main-sequence runaway star candidates at high Galactic latitudes using Gaia DR2 data, applying photometric, astrometric, and velocity criteria to minimize contamination from non-runaway stars such as blue stragglers and horizontal branch members. Galactic orbit simulation: This section describes a galactic orbit simulation using GALPY to model the past and future trajectories of Milky Way runaway main-sequence stars, incorporating observational uncertainties and calculating key orbital and ejection properties to estimate escape probabilities and ejection velocities. Properties of the runaway candidates: Most runaway main-sequence stars have Galactic velocities consistent with being bound to the Milky Way, with only a few exhibiting kinematic properties typical of horizontal-branch stars, suggesting possible classification ambiguities for those with high tangential velocities and retrograde motion.

title: Runaway blue main-sequence stars at high Galactic latitudes.

catalog_assessment.evidence: Paper describes target selection from Gaia and subsequent spectroscopic follow-up of 48 stars. It presents derived atmospheric/physical parameters (Teff, log g, RV, distance, mass, etc.) for individual objects, identifies 12 runaway MS candidates, and analyzes their trajectories and ejection velocities. The work is centered on observational analysis of a specific candidate sample.
